frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: Surprisal under Bayes' rule wrongly equated with descriptive complexity in perception.

frameworks.name: minimum description length
frameworks.one_line: Treats simplicity as shortest code length of a perceptual hypothesis.

frameworks.name: structural information theory
frameworks.one_line: Codes perceptual organizations by structural regularity to define simplicity.

core_question: Does Bayesian surprisal correctly express perceptual complexity in models of visual perceptual organization?
key_assumptions: Perceptual organization can be analyzed via formal simplicity measures
Surprisal and description length are distinct constructs
manipulations: Conceptual contrast of Bayesian, MDL, and SIT formalisms applied to grouping and figure-ground phenomena
key_findings: Surprisal (negative log probability) is not equivalent to complexity (shortest description length)
The perceptual-organization literature has conflated Bayesian, MDL, and SIT notions of simplicity
interpretations: Bayesian simplicity claims in perceptual organization rest on a mistaken identification of surprisal with complexity
Each of the three views makes distinct predictions for grouping and figure-ground experiments
